message.content:
You are reviewing an Angular app for PERFORMANCE and PAGE-LOAD issues ONLY (not correctness). Read the ACTUAL code, do not guess.

Review in C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend:
- src/app/custom-layout/ (all files)
- src/app/app.component.ts and src/app/app.module.ts
- All files under src/app/helpers/services/ and src/app/helpers/ (HTTP interceptors, auth, shared services)
- src/app/components/factory/factory.component.ts
- Any service that fetches data used app-wide on startup.

Look specifically for:
1. RxJS subscriptions never unsubscribed (memory leaks).
2. Services making HTTP calls that are NOT cached / shareReplay'd but called from many places (duplicate requests). Look for services that re-fetch the same reference data (users, vendors, categories) on every component init.
3. App-wide eager work on startup: large synchronous loops, JSON.parse of big localStorage blobs, multiple sequential HTTP calls in app.component/custom-layout init that block first render and could be parallel/deferred.
4. HTTP interceptors doing expensive synchronous work on every request.
5. app.module.ts: large eager (non-lazy) module imports that bloat the initial bundle.
6. Polling intervals that are too aggressive.

For each finding report: severity (High/Med/Low), absolute file path WITH line number, 1-2 sentence problem description citing the real code, and a concrete fix. Rank by impact. Return ONLY the findings list, no preamble. Be concrete — cite actual variable/method names and line numbers.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

41	    Settings.defaultLocale = this.localeId;

51	     * Example: example.com/?layout=apollo&style=default
52	     */
53	    this.route.queryParamMap.pipe(
54	      filter(queryParamMap => queryParamMap.has('rtl')),
55	      map(queryParamMap => coerceBooleanProperty(queryParamMap.get('rtl'))),
56	    ).subscribe(isRtl => {
57	      this.configService.updateConfig({
58	        rtl: isRtl
59	      });
60	    });
61	
62	    this.route.queryParamMap.pipe(
63	      filter(queryParamMap => queryParamMap.has('layout'))
64	    ).subscribe(queryParamMap => this.configService.setConfig(queryParamMap.get('layout') as ConfigName));
65	
66	    this.route.queryParamMap.pipe(
67	      filter(queryParamMap => queryParamMap.has('style'))
68	    ).subscribe(queryParamMap => this.styleService.setStyle(queryParamMap.get('style') as Style));
69	
70	
71	    /**
72	     * Add your own routes here
73	     */
74	    this.navigationService.items = [
75	      {
76	        type: 'dropdown',

207	    const pink = colorVariables.pink;
208	    this.document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--color-primary', pink.default.replace('rgb(', '').replace(')', ''));
209	    this.document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--color-primary-contrast', pink.contrast.replace('rgb(', '').replace(')', ''));
210	  }

content.text: The base path is `C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src`. Now let me read the interceptors, key startup services, and the factory component in parallel.

Now let me look at the app-wide startup services: config.service (APP_INITIALIZER), azure-sso, main.service, notification.service, and the helpers module. Let me read those plus check for polling and reference-data services.

Now let me look at main.service (itemService) and the reference-data services (customer, admin) plus where notification polling/mentionableUsers and reference data get called, to find duplicate-fetch patterns. Let me also check the toolbar/notification-bell component that drives startup.

Now let me look at the toolbar/notification components in custom-layout that wire startup, and check the azure-sso service and how reference data (vendors/customers/users) is consumed. Let me check the custom-layout html and toolbar, plus where notification polling starts and the @vex toolbar.

content.text:
This confirms reference-data is fetched in many components without caching. `getCustomers()` and `getAllVendors()` are called across 36 files, none using shareReplay. The CustomerService has a `customers$` BehaviorSubject store but components mostly bypass it and call `itemService.getAllVendors()`/`getCustomers()` directly.

I have enough to compile findings. Let me quickly verify the impersonation.service (injected in toolbar) for any startup work, and confirm SimpleNotificationsModule.forRoot and CalendarModule are eager.

I have enough to compile the findings.

## Performance & Page-Load Review Findings (ranked by impact)

**1. [High] Reference data (vendors / customers / RFQ groups) re-fetched on every component init, never cached/shareReplay'd**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\helpers\services\main.service.ts:63` (`getAllVendors`), `:75` (`getCustomers`), `:165` (`getRFQGroup`), `:105` (`getCompanyCode`); and `C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\helpers\services\customer.service.ts:23` (`getCustomers`). These return a fresh `http.get(...)` with no `shareReplay`, and they're called from ~36 components/dropdowns/autocompletes (70 call sites). Every RFQ/item/tracking page and every autocomplete re-downloads the full vendor & customer lists, producing many duplicate network round-trips on each navigation. CustomerService even has a `customers$` BehaviorSubject store (line 13) that most callers bypass.
Fix: Add a cached accessor per reference list, e.g. `getAllVendors$ = this.http.get(...).pipe(shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: false }))` exposed as a memoized field, and have components consume the shared observable / the existing `customers$` store instead of calling the raw HTTP method.

**2. [Med] `UserAuthInterceptor` reads `localStorage` synchronously on every single HTTP request**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\helpers\interceptor\user-auth.interceptor.ts:15` — `localStorage.getItem('token')` runs for every outbound request (and the same pattern repeats in `notification.service.ts:47`, `main.service.ts:16/22`, etc.). `localStorage` access is synchronous and forces a string read on the hot request path; with many parallel grid/data requests this adds up.
Fix: Cache the token in memory (a service field updated on login/logout) and have the interceptor read the cached value, falling back to localStorage only once.

**3. [Med] AG Grid Enterprise registers ALL modules eagerly at app bootstrap**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\app.module.ts:41-42` — `import { AllEnterpriseModule } from 'ag-grid-enterprise'; ModuleRegistry.registerModules([AllEnterpriseModule]);` runs at module-eval time. `AllEnterpriseModule` pulls the entire enterprise feature set into the initial bundle even on routes that never show a grid (login, factory form, notifications), bloating first-load JS.
Fix: Register only the specific modules actually used, or move grid module registration into the lazy-loaded grid feature modules so it's not in the initial bundle.

**4. [Med] Heavy third-party modules imported eagerly in the root AppModule**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\app.module.ts:82-98` eagerly imports `CalendarModule.forRoot(...)` (angular-calendar + date-fns adapter), `QuillModule.forRoot(...)` (rich-text editor), `CarouselModule`, `FontAwesomeModule`, and full Mat date/moment adapters (`MatMomentDateModule`, line 93). These are only needed on specific feature pages (comments editor, calendar views, carousels) but are paid for on initial load of every route including `/login`.
Fix: Move `QuillModule`, `CalendarModule`, `CarouselModule`, and the moment date adapter into the lazy feature modules that use them, keeping the root bundle lean.

**5. [Low] Popover `afterClosed$` subscriptions never unsubscribed**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\@vex\layout\toolbar\toolbar-notifications\toolbar-notifications.component.ts:49` and `C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\@vex\layout\toolbar\toolbar-user\toolbar-user.component.ts:55` — `popoverRef.afterClosed$.subscribe(...)` is called on each open with no `takeUntil`/`untilDestroyed` and no stored subscription. Each open/close cycle leaks a subscription for the toolbar's lifetime. Low impact because the toolbar is a long-lived singleton and the stream completes on close, but it's an unbounded-per-interaction pattern.
Fix: Add `untilDestroyed(this)` (the class is not currently `@UntilDestroy`) or `take(1)` on the `afterClosed$` subscription.

**6. [Low] Notification polling continues for the whole session and `getMentionableUsers` not cached**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\helpers\services\notification.service.ts:56-69` polls `/count` every 30s via `interval`, started in `toolbar.component.ts:97` and never stopped (toolbar has no `stopPolling`). 30s is reasonable, but combined with `getMentionableUsers()` (`notification.service.ts:105`) being re-fetched by the mention parser/editor on each use without caching, it adds avoidable background traffic. The polling itself is acceptable; flagging the un-cached mentionable-users fetch.
Fix: `shareReplay(1)` the mentionable-users call (it's near-static reference data), and optionally pause polling when the tab is hidden via `document.visibilitychange`.

Notes on things checked and found NOT problematic: `LoaderInterceptor` and `HttpErrorInterceptor` do only cheap header checks (no expensive sync work). `ConfigService.loadConfig` (APP_INITIALIZER) is a single small `fetch` of `config.json` — fine. `app.component.ts` constructor builds a static nav array and a few queryParam subscriptions — trivial, no blocking HTTP. `custom-layout.component.ts`/`layout.component.ts` correctly use `@UntilDestroy`/`untilDestroyed`. `UserProfileService` is a properly de-duplicated singleton store. `FactoryComponent` fetches sequentially (`listFactories` → then `loadOrders` + `loadSampleOffers`) but it is a lazy feature page, not app-wide startup, so not a page-load blocker.
